near_leaf_pairs.note: Same call on the AI CapEx cycle ($865bn since 2022 / $610bn in 2026, 'AI to remain a tailwind') expressed as a Thematic leaf by the model and a Sector leaf by GT.

Same regional AI-beneficiary call: model expressed it once at region level ('tailwind for the region'), GT decomposed the same paragraph into country rows, of which Taiwan ('clear beneficiary', IC exports +60% y/y) is the anchor; GT's South Korea and Malaysia rows are further decompositions of this same passage.

view_mismatch_judgments.reason: The source contains both an explicit positive ('we expect AI to remain a tailwind... for EMs overall', IEEPA strike-down 'positive for most countries') and explicit hedging ('conflict... clouds the near-term EM outlook', 'complex and uneven backdrop... selectivity will be critical'), so reading the net as constructive-O or balanced-N depends on whether one weights the tailwind paragraph or the selectivity framing; the article never states an allocation stance.

gt_only_root_causes.note: Source gives export data (total exports +34%, semi share >30%) with the caveat that non-semi exports are lackluster; turning that into a country equity O is a macro-to-equity inference the model didn't make.
Source says India is 'well placed' for data centers and fiscal consolidation narrowed spreads; the model captured this content under its Data Centers O row but did not infer a country equity call.
Source only states LatAm's historically elevated real rates and expected cautious BCB easing; the 'carry for local currency bond investors' framing is GT's inference, not source text.
Derived from Mexico rate-cut expectations, fiscal consolidation, and Section 122 tariff moderation; no fixed-income view is stated in the source.

Source notes APAC trade strength broadening to Malaysia and data-center positioning but also electronics employment at an eight-year low; net country equity O requires weighing the model didn't attempt.

Neutral is a synthesis of offsetting prose signals (tariff wall likely rebuilt via Sections 232/301 vs ambitious AI+ strategy and understated $67bn AI spend); the model read this content but made no call.
Source statement is about CEE central banks staying on hold and Hungary's election; mapping a rates-on-hold observation to an equities N is a multi-step (and arguably disputable) inference.

Source treats an oil-price spike as a risk scenario for energy-importing EMs, not an oil allocation view; GT's U encodes 'oil risk is negative for EMs' — a spike scenario would be bullish oil price, so the row itself is disputable and there was no candidate for the model to emit.
GT synthesizes three separate fiscal-slippage paragraphs (Colombia, Indonesia, Romania) into one sovereign U; nothing in the source states a sovereign-debt view directly.

Source describes no-confidence risk, Cambodia border tensions, weak growth, and fiscal slippage potential; converting political-risk prose into an equity U call is an inference step the model didn't take.

notes: The source is an economists' macro article with no allocation dials, tables, or explicit OW/UW language, so every GT row is an analyst inference from prose; the model emitted only 4 aggregate-level calls (AI, Asia, EM equities, Data Centers) versus GT's 14 mostly country/asset-level decompositions. The dominant gap is granularity/inference depth, not missed text — all GT-cited passages are present in snapshot.txt, which the model ingested. GT's Oil U row is the one structurally questionable ground-truth entry.
